title: Represent machine/state evolution as a sequence of `(state, output)` pairs
intent: Model a stateful encryptor as a recurrence that threads state and emits one output per input symbol.
pattern_summary: Seed a sequence with the initial state and a placeholder output, then compute one `(next_state, output)` pair per input symbol, finally projecting the outputs.
pattern_template:
pairs = [(initState, seedOut)] #
        [ step state input
        | input <- inputs
        | (state, _) <- pairs
        ]
outputs = tail [ out | (_, out) <- pairs ]
rationale: This is an idiomatic way to model finite-state machines without mutable variables.
positive_example:
rcs = [(m.rotors, '*')] #
      [ enigmaLoop (m.plugboard, r, m.reflector, c)
      | c <- pt
      | (r, _) <- rcs
      ]

enigma (m, pt) = tail [ c | (_, c) <- rcs ]
variation_notes: The threaded state may be a record rather than a tuple.
Often combines with a separate round pipeline function for one symbol/block.
anti_pattern: Hand-managing state updates outside a recurrence when the process is one-step-per-input and purely functional.
constraints: The seed output is usually a placeholder and should be removed with `tail` or equivalent projection.
The step function must consume exactly one prior state and one new input.
